news_summary: Today's technology news highlights breakthroughs in environmental monitoring with NASA's tsunami detection, advances in synthetic biology through genome transplantation in bacteria, and innovative methods for efficiently managing large data sets.
news_summary_fr: L'actualité technologique d'aujourd'hui met en avant des avancées majeures dans le domaine de la surveillance environnementale, notamment le système de détection des tsunamis de la NASA, des progrès en biologie synthétique grâce à la transplantation de génomes chez les bactéries, ainsi que des méthodes innovantes pour gérer efficacement de grands volumes de données.
news_summary_es: Las noticias tecnológicas de hoy destacan los avances en la vigilancia medioambiental con el sistema de detección de tsunamis de la NASA, los progresos en biología sintética mediante el trasplante de genomas en bacterias y los métodos innovadores para gestionar de forma eficiente grandes conjuntos de datos.

articles.title: See NASA’s GUARDIAN Catch a Tsunami
articles.summary: A new data visualization illustrates how an experimental NASA technology can provide extra lead time to communities in the path of a tsunami. Called GUARDIAN (GNSS Upper Atmospheric Real-time Disaster Information and Alert Network), the software detects slight distortions in satellite navigation signals to spot hazards on the move.. The animation breaks down a real-life […]
articles.content:
This animation shows how, following a massive earthquake off Russia on July 29, 2025, GUARDIAN flagged an incoming wave west of Hawaii some 32 minutes before it made landfall and was detected by tide gauges (shown in blue). Credit: NASA’s Scientific Visualization Studio

A new data visualization illustrates how an experimental NASA technology can provide extra lead time to communities in the path of a tsunami. Called GUARDIAN (GNSS Upper Atmospheric Real-time Disaster Information and Alert Network), the software detects slight distortions in satellite navigation signals to spot hazards on the move..

The animation breaks down a real-life case study: last summer’s massive Kamchatka earthquake and the tsunami that it sent racing across the Pacific and towards Hawaii at over 500 mph (805 kph).

The visualization shows the magnitude 8.8 earthquake (seen in purple) strike off the Russian coast on July 29, 2025, triggering the tsunami. The red, orange, yellow, and green ringlets represent real-time readings from ground stations tracking GPS and other navigational satellite signals. The disturbances were spotted by GUARDIAN’s artificial intelligence-powered detection algorithms as soon as eight minutes after the earthquake.

For the next several hours, signs of the tsunami were picked up by GUARDIAN across the Pacific Ocean in near real time. The system flagged an incoming wave off the coast of Kauai some 32 minutes before it made landfall and was detected by tide gauges (shown in blue).

The results highlight GUARDIAN’s potential to augment existing early warning systems, said Camille Martire, one of its developers at NASA’s Jet Propulsion Laboratory in Southern California.

Currently, determining whether an earthquake generated a tsunami remains a challenge. Forecasters rely on seismic data and computer simulations to make their best prediction, then wait for pressure sensors attached to the ocean floor to confirm a passing wave. Those sensors work well but are expensive and thinly dispersed. Gaps in coverage remain. And in those gaps, warning time disappears.

The GUARDIAN approach is complementary and cost effective because it monitors existing data from GPS and other constellations that make up the Global Navigation Satellite System. It’s also free to access, though for now best suited to analysts trained to interpret its findings.

How GUARDIAN works

All day, every day, geopositioning constellations transmit radio signals to ground stations around the globe. On the ground, the data is refined to sub-decimeter (less than 10 centimeters) positioning accuracy by JPL’s Global Differential GPS System. Before the signals get there, however, they must travel through an electrically charged skin of plasma called the ionosphere.

Solar storms and other space weather can wreak electrical mayhem in the ionosphere, and so can events on Earth. Tsunamis and earthquakes, by displacing large amount of air at Earth’s surface, unleash pressure waves that can slightly perturb the radio signals coming down from satellites. While systems are in place to correct for this “noise,” GUARDIAN considers it a useful signal.

Currently, GUARDIAN scours data from more than 350 GNSS ground stations around the Pacific Ring of Fire, a hotbed for the ocean’s deadliest waves. And the system is not confined to tsunamis. Earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, missile tests, spacecraft reentries, meteoroid splashdowns — anything that produces a large rumble on Earth is potentially fair game. While the Kamchatka event didn’t cause widespread damage to people or property, it showed how the next time disaster strikes, NASA science could give communities a few more minutes to act.

GUARDIAN is being developed at JPL by the GDGPS project, which is partially supported by NASA’s Space Geodesy Project.

To learn more, visit: https://guardian.jpl.nasa.gov/

Media Contacts

Andrew Wang / Andrew Good

Jet Propulsion Laboratory, Pasadena, Calif.

626-379-6874 / 818-393-2433

andrew.wang@jpl.nasa.gov / andrew.c.good@jpl.nasa.gov

Written by Sally Younger

2026-017
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant technological advancement by NASA in tsunami early warning systems using satellite signal distortions detected by GUARDIAN. This technology offers broad, tangible benefits by potentially providing extra lead time to communities at risk of tsunamis, improving disaster preparedness and saving lives. The article provides detailed context about how GUARDIAN works, its real-world application in a recent earthquake and tsunami event, and its potential to complement existing systems, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news with meaningful societal impact.
articles.personality_title: NASA’s GUARDIAN system spots tsunamis earlier using satellite signals
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Tsunamis are giant ocean waves caused by underwater earthquakes or volcanic eruptions. They can cause serious damage when reaching coastlines. Warning people early can save lives, but current systems have limits because sensors on the ocean floor are expensive and not everywhere.

**What happened** – NASA created an experimental technology called GUARDIAN (GNSS Upper Atmospheric Real-time Disaster Information and Alert Network). It watches for tiny changes in satellite signals caused by pressure waves in the atmosphere after earthquakes. In July 2025, after a big earthquake near Russia, GUARDIAN detected the tsunami heading toward Hawaii 32 minutes before it reached the shore.

**Impact** – This early detection is important because it gives people more time to prepare and stay safe. Unlike ocean sensors, GUARDIAN uses existing satellite data, which is cheaper and covers more areas. It can spot tsunamis and other big Earth events by tracking how these events change signals traveling through the ionosphere, a layer of charged particles above Earth.

**What's next step** – NASA is still developing GUARDIAN to work alongside current warning systems. They aim to expand its use around the Pacific Ring of Fire, where many tsunamis happen. In the future, it could help warn about other events like volcanic eruptions or meteor impacts.

**One-sentence takeaway** – NASA’s GUARDIAN system uses satellite signal changes to detect tsunamis earlier, offering extra warning time to protect coastal communities.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le système GUARDIAN de la NASA détecte les tsunamis plus tôt grâce aux signaux satellites
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les tsunamis sont de grandes vagues océaniques provoquées par des séismes sous-marins ou des éruptions volcaniques. Ils peuvent causer de graves dégâts en atteignant les côtes. Prévenir les populations à l'avance peut sauver des vies, mais les systèmes actuels ont des limites car les capteurs sous-marins sont coûteux et peu nombreux.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – La NASA a créé une technologie expérimentale appelée GUARDIAN (GNSS Upper Atmospheric Real-time Disaster Information and Alert Network). Elle détecte de petits changements dans les signaux satellites causés par des ondes de pression dans l’atmosphère après un séisme. En juillet 2025, après un gros séisme près de la Russie, GUARDIAN a repéré le tsunami se dirigeant vers Hawaï 32 minutes avant son arrivée.

**Impact** – Cette détection précoce est importante car elle donne plus de temps aux personnes pour se préparer et rester en sécurité. Contrairement aux capteurs océaniques, GUARDIAN utilise des données satellitaires existantes, ce qui revient moins cher et couvre plus de zones. Il peut détecter des tsunamis et d’autres grands événements terrestres en suivant comment ces événements modifient les signaux traversant l’ionosphère, une couche de particules chargées au-dessus de la Terre.

**Prochaine étape** – La NASA continue de développer GUARDIAN pour qu’il fonctionne avec les systèmes d’alerte actuels. Ils veulent étendre son usage autour de la Ceinture de feu du Pacifique, où de nombreux tsunamis se produisent. À l’avenir, il pourrait aussi aider à prévenir des événements comme des éruptions volcaniques ou des impacts de météorites.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le système GUARDIAN de la NASA utilise les changements dans les signaux satellites pour détecter les tsunamis plus tôt et offrir un temps d’alerte supplémentaire aux communautés côtières.
articles.personality_title_es: El sistema GUARDIAN de la NASA detecta tsunamis antes usando señales satelitales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los tsunamis son olas gigantes causadas por terremotos submarinos o erupciones volcánicas. Pueden causar daños graves al llegar a las costas. Avisar a las personas con tiempo puede salvar vidas, pero los sistemas actuales tienen limitaciones porque los sensores en el fondo del océano son caros y están pocos lugares.

**Qué pasó** – La NASA creó una tecnología experimental llamada GUARDIAN (Red de Información y Alerta en Tiempo Real de la Atmósfera Superior GNSS). Detecta pequeños cambios en las señales satelitales causados por ondas de presión en la atmósfera después de un terremoto. En julio de 2025, tras un gran terremoto cerca de Rusia, GUARDIAN detectó el tsunami que se acercaba a Hawái 32 minutos antes de que llegara a la costa.

**Impacto** – Esta detección temprana es importante porque da más tiempo para que las personas se preparen y estén seguras. A diferencia de los sensores oceánicos, GUARDIAN usa datos satelitales existentes, que son más baratos y cubren más áreas. Puede detectar tsunamis y otros eventos grandes en la Tierra al seguir cómo estos eventos cambian las señales que viajan por la ionosfera, una capa de partículas cargadas sobre la Tierra.

**Próximo paso** – La NASA sigue desarrollando GUARDIAN para que funcione junto con los sistemas de alerta actuales. Quieren ampliar su uso alrededor del Cinturón de Fuego del Pacífico, donde ocurren muchos tsunamis. En el futuro, también podría ayudar a alertar sobre otros eventos como erupciones volcánicas o impactos de meteoritos.

**Resumen en una frase** – El sistema GUARDIAN de la NASA usa cambios en señales satelitales para detectar tsunamis antes, dando tiempo extra para proteger a las comunidades costeras.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast Pacific Ocean scene at dawn, featuring a stylized purple earthquake ripple emanating from the Kamchatka coast, concentric rings of red, orange, yellow, and green waves symbolizing GUARDIAN’s satellite signal detections spreading across the water, and gentle blue tide gauge markers near the Hawaiian islands, all rendered with soft, natural colors and fluid, harmonious lines to evoke calm vigilance and advanced technology protecting communities.

articles.title: 'Zombie' cells created by transplanting genomes into dead bacteria
articles.summary: Researchers have created the first living synthetic bacterium made from non-living parts by killing a bacterial cell and then transplanting the genome of another species into it, blurring the boundary between life and death
articles.content:
Researchers have created the first living synthetic bacterium made from non-living parts by killing a bacterial cell and then transplanting the genome of another species into it, blurring the boundary between life and death

Colonies of bacterial cells under a microscope. The blue colony is expressing the synthetic genome; the white colonies are Mycoplasma capricolum cells that survived the mitomycin C treatment Nacyra Assad-Garcia

A living, synthetic cell has been made by transplanting a complete genome into a dead bacterium, bringing it back to life. The breakthrough could help synthetic biology live up to its huge, but still distant, promise of engineering organisms to create sustainable fuels, pharmaceuticals and new materials.

Synthetic biology involves tweaking biological systems or creating new ones to introduce novel functions, such as rewriting yeast DNA so that the organisms make desirable chemicals. In an effort to make more versatile engineered microbes, in 2010 researchers synthesised a bacterial genome and then transplanted it into a living cell, creating what they called the first synthetic cell.

But there was a problem. It was very difficult to be sure whether the cell was truly being governed by the synthetic genome rather than its original genome, because bacteria frequently absorb genetic material from the environment and add it to their own genomes in a process called horizontal gene transfer.

To get around this issue, John Glass at the J. Craig Venter Institute (JCVI) in La Jolla, California, and his colleagues decided to kill the host cell first – or at least its genome.

The researchers turned to a chemical called mitomycin C, used as a chemotherapy drug to kill cancer cells by damaging their DNA, and tried it on cells of the simple bacterium Mycoplasma capricolum.

Free newsletter Sign up to The Earth Edition Unmissable news about our planet, delivered straight to your inbox each month. Sign up to newsletter

“The cell is still healthy but since it cannot reproduce any more and the genome is not functional any more, it is destined to die or it’s already dead,” says team member Zumra Seidel, also at the JCVI.

Next, they added a synthetic version of the genome of another bacterium, Mycoplasma mycoides, to the dead cells using a technique they call whole-genome transplantation.

Some of the bacteria began to grow and divide normally and genetic tests showed they carried the synthetic genome. This makes them the first living, synthetic bacterial cells constructed from non-living parts, claim the researchers, who call them “zombie cells” because they have been revived after death.

“We take a cell without a genome and it is functionally dead. But by adding a new genome, that cell is resurrected,” says Glass.

Kate Adamala at the University of Minnesota calls the work a technical breakthrough. “They’re putting a genome payload into a non-living recipient, so they’re getting no help from the host’s own repair mechanisms. They essentially booted that cell back up,” she says. “It’s amazing work.”

It also blurs the line between life and non-life, says Adamala. “The business model of any proper living cell is to metabolise and replicate. Those functionalities became the hallmark of life. The recipient [cell’s genome] in this does very little of residual metabolising and is most certainly not replicating. What is then a true hallmark of life?”

Team member Elizabeth Strychalski at the National Institute of Standards and Technology in Gaithersburg, Maryland, suggests that biology may routinely operate across a porous boundary between life and death. “I hope this prompts people to think about how life is a series of processes and if we bring an engineering mindset to that, we can look at our living system and ask which processes we really need for the end goal that we’re trying to accomplish.”

So far, the technique has only been tried on Mycoplasma, but the team sees it as proof of principle that could enable faster creation of synthetic organisms to work as mini chemicals factories, making therapeutic drugs or performing environmental remediation.

“For a long time, we have had the ability to assemble very large pieces of synthetic DNA, but we haven’t been able to deliver them to a place where they can do useful things,” says Strychalski. “It’s like having a script for a Shakespeare play, but not being able to actually perform it.”

Akos Nyerges at Harvard Medical School says the work addresses a major challenge in synthetic biology. “This technology makes genome transfer a more predictable and reliable strategy, which potentially opens up a lot of follow-up applications in other species,” he says.

Moving towards more complex organisms like yeast or E. coli may be challenging, because these organisms have a cell wall, which Mycoplasma lacks, and bigger genomes, but Glass is optimistic the technique will succeed in them, too.

“If this works for one type of organism, it is likely to work for another,” he says, and his lab is investigating ways to remove and replace cell walls. “In the right growth conditions, E. coli will make a new cell wall,” he says.

There is always the possibility of biosafety issues with synthetic biology, says Nyerges. The Mycoplasma species used in the study are pathogens in goats and cattle, but he says none of the modifications are expected to increase virulence.

Strychalski says existing laboratory best practices make sure the risk of pathogens escaping is minimal.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in synthetic biology where researchers created living synthetic bacterial cells from non-living parts by transplanting genomes into dead bacteria. This achievement has broad implications for engineering organisms to produce sustainable fuels, pharmaceuticals, and environmental remediation, representing a meaningful advance with potential large-scale benefits. The article provides detailed context about the method, challenges overcome, and future applications, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create living bacteria from dead cells using genome transplant
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long worked to build synthetic cells by changing or adding DNA to living bacteria. In 2010, researchers made a synthetic cell by putting a new genome into a living bacterium, but it was hard to tell if the new DNA really controlled the cell because bacteria can swap genes naturally.

**What happened** – A team at the J. Craig Venter Institute in California used a chemical called mitomycin C to kill the DNA inside bacterial cells of Mycoplasma capricolum, leaving the cells alive but unable to reproduce. Then they transplanted a complete synthetic genome from another bacterium, Mycoplasma mycoides, into these “dead” cells. Some of these bacteria started growing and dividing normally again, showing the new genome was in control. The researchers called these revived cells “zombie cells.”

**Impact** – This is the first time living synthetic bacteria have been made from non-living parts. It proves that a genome alone can “bring back to life” a cell that had lost its DNA. This method avoids the problem of natural gene swapping and could speed up making engineered microbes for making medicines, fuels, or cleaning the environment. It also challenges ideas about what makes something truly alive.

**What's next step** – The team plans to try this technique on more complex bacteria like E. coli, which have cell walls and larger genomes. They are exploring how to remove and replace cell walls to apply genome transplantation more broadly. If successful, this could open many new uses in synthetic biology and biotechnology.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists revived dead bacterial cells by inserting new genomes, creating the first living synthetic bacteria made entirely from non-living parts.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent des bactéries vivantes à partir de cellules mortes grâce à une transplantation de génome
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques travaillent depuis longtemps à construire des cellules synthétiques en modifiant ou en ajoutant de l’ADN à des bactéries vivantes. En 2010, des chercheurs ont créé une cellule synthétique en insérant un nouveau génome dans une bactérie vivante, mais il était difficile de savoir si le nouvel ADN contrôlait vraiment la cellule, car les bactéries échangent naturellement des gènes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de l’Institut J. Craig Venter en Californie a utilisé un produit chimique appelé mitomycine C pour tuer l’ADN à l’intérieur de cellules bactériennes de Mycoplasma capricolum, laissant les cellules vivantes mais incapables de se reproduire. Ensuite, ils ont transplanté un génome synthétique complet d’une autre bactérie, Mycoplasma mycoides, dans ces cellules « mortes ». Certaines bactéries ont recommencé à croître et à se diviser normalement, montrant que le nouveau génome contrôlait la cellule. Les chercheurs ont appelé ces cellules ressuscitées « cellules zombies ».

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que des bactéries synthétiques vivantes sont créées à partir de parties non vivantes. Cela prouve qu’un génome seul peut « ramener à la vie » une cellule qui avait perdu son ADN. Cette méthode évite le problème de l’échange naturel de gènes et pourrait accélérer la création de microbes conçus pour fabriquer des médicaments, des carburants ou nettoyer l’environnement. Cela remet aussi en question ce qui définit vraiment la vie.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit d’essayer cette technique sur des bactéries plus complexes comme E. coli, qui ont une paroi cellulaire et un génome plus grand. Ils explorent comment retirer et remplacer les parois cellulaires pour appliquer plus largement la transplantation de génome. Si cela réussit, cela pourrait ouvrir de nombreuses nouvelles applications en biologie synthétique.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des scientifiques ont ressuscité des cellules bactériennes mortes en insérant de nouveaux génomes, créant les premières bactéries synthétiques vivantes faites entièrement de parties non vivantes.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean bacterias vivas a partir de células muertas usando trasplante de genoma
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han trabajado durante mucho tiempo en construir células sintéticas modificando o agregando ADN a bacterias vivas. En 2010, los investigadores crearon una célula sintética al poner un nuevo genoma en una bacteria viva, pero era difícil saber si el nuevo ADN realmente controlaba la célula porque las bacterias intercambian genes de forma natural.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo del Instituto J. Craig Venter en California usó una sustancia química llamada mitomicina C para matar el ADN dentro de células bacterianas de Mycoplasma capricolum, dejando las células vivas pero incapaces de reproducirse. Luego trasplantaron un genoma sintético completo de otra bacteria, Mycoplasma mycoides, en estas células “muertas”. Algunas bacterias comenzaron a crecer y dividirse normalmente, mostrando que el nuevo genoma estaba en control. Los investigadores llamaron a estas células resucitadas “células zombis”.

**Impacto** – Esta es la primera vez que se crean bacterias sintéticas vivas a partir de partes no vivas. Esto demuestra que un genoma por sí solo puede “revivir” una célula que había perdido su ADN. Este método evita el problema del intercambio natural de genes y podría acelerar la creación de microbios diseñados para producir medicinas, combustibles o limpiar el medio ambiente. También cuestiona qué es lo que realmente define la vida.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea probar esta técnica en bacterias más complejas como E. coli, que tienen pared celular y genomas más grandes. Están explorando cómo eliminar y reemplazar las paredes celulares para aplicar el trasplante de genoma más ampliamente. Si tiene éxito, podría abrir muchas nuevas aplicaciones en biología sintética.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos revivieron células bacterianas muertas insertando nuevos genomas, creando las primeras bacterias sintéticas vivas hechas completamente de partes no vivas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of translucent bacterial cells under a microscope, with one cell glowing softly in blue to symbolize the synthetic genome bringing it to life, surrounded by pale white cells representing the dead hosts, all set against a muted natural background that evokes a microscopic world bridging life and death.

articles.title: Drowning in data sets? Here’s how to cut them down to size
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 23 March 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-00880-7Indefinite data retention is neither financially nor practically possible, but there are ways to give your data maximal long-term value.
articles.content:
Within the next decade, a pair of giant radio telescopes in South Africa and Australia will be able to generate about 700 petabytes of data each year, the equivalent of about 149 million DVDs, a stack nearly 180 kilometres high.

The telescopes are part of the Square Kilometre Array Observatory (SKAO), which will include more than 100,000 Christmas-tree-like wire antennas in Australia and some 200 dishes in South Africa when it is completed in 2029. These telescopes will pick up radio signals from celestial objects, and their developers hope that they will shed light on some of astronomy’s long-standing questions, such as what dark matter is and how galaxies form.

Microsoft team creates ‘revolutionary’ data-storage system that lasts for millennia

But 700 petabytes is only about 1% of the data that the array could generate. Shari Breen, head of science operations at the SKAO in Jodrell Bank, UK, estimates that it could produce some 60 exabytes — 60,000 petabytes — each year if researchers used all of its systems continuously and retained all of the data.

“The amount of money that it would take to hold our rawest forms of data is insane — I don’t even know where we would fit that many computers,” says Breen. “So, we have to make some compromises.”

Disciplines such as astronomy and the Earth and biological sciences have long grappled with unwieldy data sets. As the volume, processing speed and variety of data continue to grow, the storage capacity is struggling to keep pace. At the same time, the boom in machine-learning and artificial-intelligence technologies is creating an incentive to hoard information. But unconstrained data retention is not financially viable and uses a great deal of energy.

“This is a problem that libraries have been dealing with for as long as libraries have existed,” says Kristin Briney, a librarian at the California Institute of Technology (Caltech) in Pasadena. “We cannot physically collect all the books that we want to collect, and in 50 years, the book may not be useful any more.”

Data sets, she says, are the same. “There has to be some curation that determines what is worth keeping and what is worth throwing away.”

Field-specific rules

There is no one-size-fits-all rulebook for data curation, and best practice often depends on the discipline and on the scale of a project.

The SKAO, for instance, will store the products that it makes according to what the scientists ask for in advance, says Breen. The products can range from raw data to highly processed images. So if an astronomer requests an image based on interferometry data, then the underlying data set will be discarded once the picture’s quality has been deemed sufficient, she says.

Breen, who is a principal investigator on a large astronomical survey, says that in the past, she would request raw data. “Now, I’m like, ‘No, please don’t!’,” she says. “The reality of these next-generation telescopes is that then you’ll spend all your time bogged down by enormous data sets rather than delivering the awesome science that was the whole point.” Instead, she typically asks for an interactive 3D array of pixels known as an image cube, which is easier to wrangle, she says.

‘Google for DNA’ brings order to biology’s big data

Meteorologists, by contrast, still prefer to work with the raw data. The World Meteorological Organization (WMO) receives data from thousands of satellites, marine platforms, aerial surveys and ground-based stations around the world, which record parameters such as atmospheric pressure, wind speed, air temperature and humidity, often hourly.

“We have a principle in meteorology, which is that we have to archive all the original data in order to enable us to always produce any product we have ever produced out of the original data,” says WMO scientific officer Peer Hechler in Geneva, Switzerland. The meteorology community uses original data to create projections and models, but “it doesn’t make sense economically to store all these derivative data sets”, he says.

Similarly, the Wellcome Sanger Institute, a genomics research organization in Hinxton, UK, keeps most of the raw data it generates, says sequencing informatics team leader David Jackson. Its DNA database already contains some 90 petabytes of data. As a result, Jackson says, the organization needs clear data-retention policies, and soon. “You get to the point where the data becomes more of a liability than an asset,” he says.

What needs to be kept

Whatever the discipline, the first step in managing massive data sets is working out what needs to be kept and what can be thrown away. Although practices vary, librarians and data specialists say that there are some overarching principles.

Some data sets must be kept because they are irreplaceable or legal requisites. Others might have been used in a publication or for a government decision, and need to be stored so that future readers can see the evidence on which a decision was based.

‘Do-it-yourself’ data storage on DNA paves way to simple archiving system

Many funders, including the US National Institutes of Health, require that data remain available to other researchers. To do so, researchers can use shared repositories such as the generalist Zenodo and Dryad databases, or more specialist systems, including the Open Data Commons for Spinal Cord Injury. The Registry of Research Data Repositories provides an index of nearly 3,500 such resources.

The US National Science Foundation requires grant recipients to submit a data-management plan, including information about the size and storage plans for data sets, as well as how much of the grant will be allocated to this. It offers guidance that is tailored to different disciplines. For instance, the guidelines for biological sciences contain information about how to handle sensitive data relating to human participants, whereas those for mathematics have provisions for making code and software open source and contain suggestions about data formats.

The UK National Environment Research Council has developed a checklist that covers the data’s legal status, potential reuse and historical and scientific value, says Sam Pepler, curation manager at the Centre for Environmental Data Analysis in Leicester, UK. The list could be useful for other fields of research, too, Pepler says, but he cautions that it is subjective and that disciplines often have their own requirements.

One thing that is not subjective, however, is the importance of the metadata that describe the data set. Helen Glaves, a senior data scientist at the British Geological Survey in Nottingham, UK, says that metadata are “absolutely fundamental”. If data sets have poor metadata, she explains, their value for reuse might be limited.
articles.reasoning: The article discusses innovative approaches to managing and curating massive scientific data sets generated by next-generation telescopes and other research fields. It highlights the development of data-storage strategies that optimize long-term value, reduce financial and energy costs, and enable continued scientific discovery. This has broad significance for multiple scientific disciplines and society's ability to handle big data sustainably.
articles.personality_title: New ways to manage huge science data sets save space and money
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the coming years, giant telescopes in South Africa and Australia will create enormous amounts of data—so much that storing all of it is impossible and very expensive. Scientists from many fields face this challenge as data grows faster than storage technology.

**What happened** – The Square Kilometre Array Observatory (SKAO), with thousands of antennas and dishes, will produce up to 700 petabytes of data each year by 2029. That’s like stacking 149 million DVDs nearly 180 kilometers high. Experts say storing all raw data isn’t practical or affordable, so they are developing ways to keep only the most useful information, like processed images instead of raw signals.

**Impact** – These new data-management methods help scientists focus on important results without getting lost in endless data. Different fields, like astronomy, meteorology, and biology, choose what to keep based on their needs. For example, meteorologists keep all original data to make weather models, while astronomers often keep only processed images. This careful approach saves money, energy, and space while still supporting important research.

**What’s next step** – Researchers will continue creating and improving rules to decide what data is worth saving. Many funders now require clear plans for managing data during projects. Shared databases and detailed descriptions (called metadata) help others find and reuse valuable data. These efforts will help science handle growing data in a smarter, more sustainable way.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists are developing smart ways to store and manage huge data sets to save resources and support future discoveries across many fields.
articles.personality_title_fr: De nouvelles méthodes pour gérer d’énormes ensembles de données scientifiques économisent espace et argent
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dans les prochaines années, de gigantesques télescopes en Afrique du Sud et en Australie vont produire d’énormes quantités de données, si importantes qu’il est impossible et très coûteux de toutes les stocker. Les scientifiques de nombreux domaines font face à ce défi, car les données croissent plus vite que la technologie de stockage.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’Observatoire Square Kilometre Array (SKAO), avec des milliers d’antennes et de paraboles, produira jusqu’à 700 pétaoctets de données chaque année d’ici 2029. Cela équivaut à empiler 149 millions de DVD sur près de 180 kilomètres de haut. Les experts expliquent qu’il n’est pas pratique ni abordable de conserver toutes les données brutes, alors ils développent des méthodes pour ne garder que les informations les plus utiles, comme des images traitées au lieu des signaux bruts.

**Impact** – Ces nouvelles méthodes de gestion des données aident les scientifiques à se concentrer sur les résultats importants sans se perdre dans une masse infinie de données. Différents domaines, comme l’astronomie, la météorologie et la biologie, choisissent ce qu’ils conservent selon leurs besoins. Par exemple, les météorologues conservent toutes les données originales pour créer des modèles météo, tandis que les astronomes gardent souvent seulement des images traitées. Cette approche prudente permet d’économiser de l’argent, de l’énergie et de l’espace tout en soutenant la recherche importante.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs continueront à créer et améliorer les règles pour décider quelles données méritent d’être conservées. De nombreux financeurs exigent désormais des plans clairs pour gérer les données pendant les projets. Des bases de données partagées et des descriptions détaillées (appelées métadonnées) aident les autres à trouver et réutiliser les données précieuses. Ces efforts aideront la science à gérer la croissance des données de manière plus intelligente et durable.

**Phrase clé** – Les scientifiques développent des méthodes intelligentes pour stocker et gérer d’énormes ensembles de données afin d’économiser des ressources et soutenir les découvertes futures dans de nombreux domaines.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevas formas de gestionar grandes conjuntos de datos científicos ahorran espacio y dinero
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En los próximos años, grandes telescopios en Sudáfrica y Australia generarán enormes cantidades de datos, tan grandes que almacenarlos todos es imposible y muy costoso. Científicos de varios campos enfrentan este reto porque los datos crecen más rápido que la tecnología de almacenamiento.

**Qué pasó** – El Observatorio Square Kilometre Array (SKAO), con miles de antenas y platos, producirá hasta 700 petabytes de datos cada año para 2029. Eso equivale a apilar 149 millones de DVDs casi 180 kilómetros de alto. Los expertos dicen que no es práctico ni económico guardar todos los datos sin procesar, por lo que desarrollan formas de conservar solo la información más útil, como imágenes procesadas en lugar de señales crudas.

**Impacto** – Estos nuevos métodos de manejo de datos ayudan a los científicos a enfocarse en resultados importantes sin perderse en una cantidad infinita de datos. Diferentes campos, como astronomía, meteorología y biología, eligen qué conservar según sus necesidades. Por ejemplo, los meteorólogos guardan todos los datos originales para hacer modelos del clima, mientras que los astrónomos suelen conservar solo imágenes procesadas. Este enfoque cuidadoso ahorra dinero, energía y espacio, apoyando la investigación importante.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán creando y mejorando reglas para decidir qué datos vale la pena conservar. Muchos financiadores ahora exigen planes claros para manejar los datos durante los proyectos. Bases de datos compartidas y descripciones detalladas (llamadas metadatos) ayudan a otros a encontrar y reutilizar datos valiosos. Estos esfuerzos ayudarán a la ciencia a manejar el crecimiento de datos de forma más inteligente y sostenible.

**Frase clave** – Los científicos están desarrollando formas inteligentes de almacenar y gestionar enormes conjuntos de datos para ahorrar recursos y apoyar futuros descubrimientos en varios campos.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of two vast, glowing radio telescope arrays—one with intricate wire-frame antennas shaped like stylized Christmas trees in a gently rolling Australian landscape, and the other with large, softly illuminated dish antennas on a serene South African plain—connected by streams of shimmering, flowing data threads that gracefully condense into manageable, glowing cubes floating above, symbolizing the careful curation and transformation of immense cosmic data into meaningful scientific insights.
